Prefer `unknown` over `any` when data crosses a trust boundary.
`any` silences the checker. `unknown` makes you earn your assumptions.
Turn on `strict` and let TypeScript be annoying now so prod can be quiet later.
Discriminated unions beat optional-property soup almost every time.
If a value can be one of several shapes, give it a `kind` field and narrow on that.
Use `satisfies` when you want validation without losing literal inference.
`as const` is great for config objects, route maps, and finite sets of values.
Derive unions from data when possible: `typeof arr[number]` is your friend.
Prefer deriving types from real values over duplicating shapes by hand.
A type alias plus a runtime constant is usually better than an enum-shaped detour.
Use `Record<K, V>` when keys are known and values are consistent.
Reach for `Pick` and `Omit` before creating yet another almost-identical interface.
`Partial<T>` is for updates, not for avoiding decisions.
`Required<T>` is handy when a partially-built object becomes a fully-built one.
`Readonly<T>` pairs nicely with immutable data and fewer surprise mutations.
`Awaited<T>` models async results better than hand-rolled promise peeling.
`ReturnType<typeof fn>` keeps helper types glued to real code.
`Parameters<typeof fn>` is perfect when wrappers should mirror the wrapped function.
Use `NonNullable<T>` when the null-check already happened and the type should admit it.
`Extract` and `Exclude` are scalpels for unions. Use them like it.
Template literal types turn string conventions into type-safe APIs.
If string formats matter, encode them in a template literal type instead of a README prayer.
Labeled tuples make function signatures easier to read in editor hints.
Type predicates are great when runtime checks and compile-time narrowing should agree.
If you wrote a custom guard, make it return `value is X` so callers get the payoff.
Use exhaustive `switch` statements plus `never` to make missing cases loud.
If a union grows silently, your exhaustiveness check should start screaming.
Narrow with `in`, `typeof`, and `instanceof` before reaching for assertions.
Assertions should document certainty, not bypass uncertainty.
If you keep writing `as Foo`, the real bug may be upstream in your data flow.
Prefer small generic helpers over giant generic frameworks.
A type parameter used only once is often a smell, not a flex.
Type inference is usually better when you remove annotations instead of adding more.
When inference gets too clever, `NoInfer<T>` can restore a sane source of truth.
Use `NoInfer` when one argument should follow another, not redefine it.
Constrain generics with `extends` when the function actually needs structure.
Unconstrained generics are just `mystery meat` with angle brackets.
Prefer unions over overloads unless the call shapes are truly distinct.
If overloads start reading like fan fiction, a generic probably wanted the job.
Use `import type` for type-only imports, especially with isolated builds.
Modern TS is happier when runtime imports and type imports are clearly separated.
If an import exists only for types, say so. Future tooling will thank you.
`verbatimModuleSyntax` helps your emitted code match what you actually wrote.
For ESM projects, align TS config with reality: `module` and resolution should match the runtime or bundler.
`NodeNext` is for Node ESM/CJS realism; `bundler` is for bundler realism. Pick one on purpose.
If your package uses `exports`, make sure your types story matches it too.
Use `moduleResolution: bundler` when your bundler resolves paths, not Node at runtime.
Avoid legacy compiler flags unless you enjoy archaeology.
`noUncheckedIndexedAccess` makes dictionary lookups honest.
`exactOptionalPropertyTypes` clarifies the difference between 'missing' and 'undefined'.
Optional properties mean 'may be absent' — not 'probably there if we believe hard enough.'
Array indexing can return `undefined`; your types should stop pretending otherwise.
If object keys are dynamic, model that explicitly instead of hoping the index exists.
Literal inference matters most in config-heavy code.
A config object validated with `satisfies` keeps both safety and autocomplete.
When keys must be exact, `satisfies Record<KeyUnion, Value>` catches typos beautifully.
A misspelled config key is just a bug with good branding.
Use `const` assertions for finite option lists, action types, and route names.
Derive `type Route = keyof typeof routes` instead of rewriting strings in two places.
Model impossible states as impossible types.
Many optional props are a signal that one type should really be several types.
If two fields depend on each other, encode the dependency in the type instead of a comment.
Prefer composition of small types over inheritance ladders nobody asked for.
A union of precise objects is often clearer than one mega-interface with caveats.
Use branded types when two strings are technically strings but semantically not interchangeable.
`string` and `UserId` should not be the same thing if bugs care about the difference.
Runtime validation and TypeScript types solve different problems. Use both when input is untrusted.
TypeScript does not validate API responses at runtime. It just gives you confidence, sometimes irresponsibly.
Parse unknown JSON into known shapes instead of casting and wishing.
`JSON.parse` returns `any` energy even when the type annotation looks confident.
Default generic parameters can simplify APIs without losing flexibility.
Inference is strongest when function parameters describe the source of truth clearly.
Use helper types to name complex mapped or conditional types before they become line noise.
If a conditional type needs a paragraph of explanation, extract it and give it a decent name.
Mapped types are easier to trust when the key set is small and obvious.
Use intrinsic string utilities like `Uppercase` and `Capitalize` when string conventions are part of the contract.
Template string types are great for event names, cache keys, and CSS variable contracts.
Prefer `readonly` arrays for inputs you don’t intend to mutate.
A function that promises not to mutate should accept `readonly T[]` when it can.
In TS 5.4, narrowing after the last assignment in closures got smarter. The compiler finally trusts your timing a little more.
If a closure loses narrowing, check whether the variable is reassigned later.
Use `Object.groupBy` and `Map.groupBy` with the right lib settings if your runtime supports them.
If a modern JS feature errors in TS, check `target` and `lib` before blaming the language.
Type-only import conflicts now surface more clearly. Rename or use `import type`.
If a local value and a type import want the same name, one of them needs better taste.
Prefer plain objects plus `as const` over enums when runtime output should stay minimal.
Enums are real runtime objects. Use them only when you actually want that.
String unions compose better with modern TS than most enums do.
A `never` branch in production code is a smoke alarm for future refactors.
Compiler errors are often design feedback wearing a fake mustache.
Use the editor quick fixes, but read what they changed before committing your soul.
Remove unused imports regularly. Dead imports are clutter with good PR etiquette.
Keep exported types intentional. Your public API is a promise, not a junk drawer.
If a helper’s implementation is simpler than its type, simplify the type.
Readable types beat clever types when a teammate has to debug them at 6 PM.
Sometimes the best TypeScript improvement is deleting half the generics.
A function returning a discriminated union is often nicer than throwing for expected states.
Use `Promise<Result>` shapes deliberately; async code deserves typed success and failure paths.
When a value can fail validation, represent that in the type instead of in team folklore.
Modern TypeScript rewards clarity more than wizardry — and wizardry still loses to `strict`.
If TypeScript feels like it’s fighting you, ask whether the model is wrong before assuming the checker is.
The best TS tip is still this: make illegal states unrepresentable, then go home earlier.
TypeScript is a superset of JavaScript, so migration usually starts with valid code and better options.
Let literals infer obvious types; annotations are for ambiguity, not decoration.
`number[]` and `Array<number>` are both valid — pick one style and stop starting holy wars.
Use unions like `string | number` when a function truly accepts multiple shapes.
Interfaces are structural: if it has the right shape, it fits.
Structural typing is convenient right until two unrelated shapes become accidentally compatible.
Interfaces can describe callable functions, not just object bags.
For function types, parameter names don’t matter. The types do.
Class members are public by default, so add visibility modifiers only when they clarify intent.
Constructor parameter properties are a great way to kill boilerplate without killing readability.
If a class `implements` an interface, missing members should fail fast at compile time.
Derived classes must call `super()` before acting like the base class owes them anything.
Generic helpers shine when they connect input and output types in a way overloads can’t express cleanly.
`readonly` is a compile-time guard, not a deep freeze spell.
A readonly alias can still observe mutations through a mutable reference. Immutability is a team sport.
`ReadonlyArray<T>` is perfect when consumers should iterate, not mutate.
Use `for...of` for array values and `for...in` for keys. Mixing them up is a classic side quest.
Template strings improve runtime strings; template literal types improve compile-time strings.
The TypeScript Playground is the fastest place to test a type idea before it escapes into your codebase.
Share Playground links when discussing tricky types; screenshots of red squiggles are barbarism.
Modern TS rarely needs triple-slash references in app code. Prefer package-managed types and imports.
If you start with `{}` and then assert it into shape, ask whether a proper initializer would tell the truth better.
